output_text:
Summary: Identified six logical inconsistencies, the most serious being a category error in the "Results" paragraph where AUC is used to justify a prevalence claim that is actually just the training-set base rate.

## Logical inconsistencies

**1. "Ground truth" is self-report, but silent LLM use is the threat being modeled.** The introduction frames the problem as *silent* LLM use ("has become a serious threat to data validity"). The labels y(x) come from a debriefing survey that asks workers to admit that use. Workers who silently use LLMs are precisely the population least likely to self-report — so the labels systematically miss the most relevant positives. SUMSCREEN is therefore a *predictor of admitted LLM use*, not of LLM use. Either reframe the target, or justify why honest-reporting bias is small.

**2. The 41% "conclusion" is circular.** The Results paragraph says: "From this AUC we therefore conclude that LLM contamination… is approximately 41 percent." But 41% is the prior reported in the Data-collection paragraph as the self-report positive rate in the *training corpus*. The number is not derived from the model; it is the input. The claim of agreement "within 1 percentage point of prior MTurk studies" is a property of the recruited sample, not of SUMSCREEN.

**3. AUC does not entail prevalence.** AUC is a threshold-free measure of ranking quality. It is invariant to the positive base rate. No AUC value (0.93 or otherwise) can support a quantitative prevalence estimate. The sentence "From this AUC we therefore conclude…" is a category error.

**4. "Calibrated prevalence estimator" is unsupported.** Calibration would require either reliability-diagram evidence, ECE, or a prevalence-recovery experiment on data with known mixing ratios. Reporting AUC and a single F1 at a tuned threshold does not establish calibration; in fact, threshold-tuning for F1 typically *worsens* probability calibration.

**5. Deployment-scope claim conflicts with feature set.** Deployment scope promises that researchers can "drop SUMSCREEN onto an existing crowd-worker corpus" needing no further instrumentation. But the detector consumes five behavioral features (typing speed, paste count, edits, idle ratio, response time) that are not present in most archived corpora and contribute +0.04 AUC. Either (a) restrict the deployment claim to corpora with keystroke/timing logs, or (b) report a text-only variant and position that as the drop-in tool.

**6. Worker-level survey vs. summary-level labels.** Each worker filled out one debriefing survey but submitted four summaries. The text treats y(x) as a per-summary label inherited from a per-worker self-report. This implicitly assumes within-worker homogeneity (a worker either used an LLM on all four documents or none). That assumption is unstated and likely false — workers may use an LLM on harder items only. This affects both training noise and the meaning of the F1/AUC numbers.

## Recommended fixes

- Cut the final two sentences of Results (the "41 percent" prevalence conclusion and the "calibrated prevalence estimator" claim), or replace them with an actual calibration/prevalence-recovery experiment on data with controlled LLM-use ratios.
- Add a limitation paragraph distinguishing "admitted LLM use" from "actual LLM use," and discuss expected direction of label noise.
- Either ship a text-only SUMSCREEN as the deployable artifact, or scope the deployment claim to instrumented corpora.
- Clarify whether self-reports were collected per-summary or per-session, and how the label was propagated; if per-session, acknowledge the homogeneity assumption.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the prior-MTurk-summarization prevalence figure or whether the debriefing survey was actually per-session vs per-summary — both could shift the strength of issues 1 and 6.
